    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 24
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/24](https://www.erdosproblems.com/24)
    23→
    24→Every triangle-free graph on $5n$ vertices contains at most $n^5$ copies of $C_5$.
    25→Proved independently by Grzesik [Gr12] and by Hatami, Hladký, Král, Norine, and
    26→Razborov [HHKNR13].
    27→
    28→[Gr12] Grzesik, A., _On the maximum number of five-cycles in a triangle-free graph_.
    29→Journal of Combinatorial Theory, Series B (2012), 1061-1066.
    30→
    31→[HHKNR13] Hatami, H., Hladký, J., Král, D., Norine, S., and Razborov, A.,
    32→_On the number of pentagons in triangle-free graphs_.
    33→Journal of Combinatorial Theory, Series A (2013), 722-732.
    34→-/
    35→
    36→open SimpleGraph Finset
    37→
    38→namespace Erdos24
    39→
    40→/--
    41→Every triangle-free graph on $5n$ vertices contains at most $n^5$ copies of $C_5$.
    42→
    43→We count labeled $5$-cycles: injective functions $f : \text{Fin}\,5 \to \text{Fin}(5n)$ such
    44→that $f(i)$ is adjacent to $f(i+1 \bmod 5)$ for all $i$. Each unordered $C_5$ yields exactly
    45→$10$ labeled $5$-cycles ($5$ rotations $\times$ $2$ reflections), so the labeled count bound
    46→is $10 \cdot n^5$.
    47→
    48→Proved independently by Grzesik [Gr12] and by Hatami, Hladký, Král, Norine, and
    49→Razborov [HHKNR13].
    50→-/
    51→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    52→theorem erdos_24 :
    53→    ∀ (n : ℕ) (G : SimpleGraph (Fin (5 * n))) (h : DecidableRel G.Adj),
    54→    haveI := h
    55→    G.CliqueFree 3 →
    56→    (Finset.univ.filter (fun (f : Fin 5 → Fin (5 * n)) =>
    57→      Function.Injective f ∧
    58→      ∀ i : Fin 5, G.Adj (f i) (f (i + 1)))).card
    59→    ≤ 10 * n ^ 5 := by
    60→  sorry
    61→
    62→end Erdos24
    63→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 23
    21→
    22→*References:*
    23→* [erdosproblems.com/23](https://www.erdosproblems.com/23)
    24→* [OEIS A389646](https://oeis.org/A389646)
    25→-/
    26→
    27→open SimpleGraph BigOperators Classical
    28→
    29→namespace Erdos23
    30→
    31→/--
    32→Every triangle-free graph on $5$ vertices can be made bipartite by removing at most $1$ edge.
    33→This is the $n = 1$ case of Erdős Problem 23.
    34→-/
    35→@[category test, AMS 5]
    36→theorem erdos_23_n1 :
    37→    ∀ (G : SimpleGraph (Fin 5)), G.CliqueFree 3 → ∃ (H : SimpleGraph (Fin 5)),
    38→        H ≤ G ∧ H.IsBipartite ∧ (G.edgeFinset \ H.edgeFinset).card ≤ 1 := by
    39→  sorry
    40→
    41→/--
    42→There exists a triangle-free graph on $5$ vertices such that at least $1$ edge must be removed
    43→to make it bipartite. This shows the bound in `erdos_23_n1` is tight.
    44→-/
    45→@[category test, AMS 5]
    46→theorem erdos_23_n1_tight :
    47→    ∃ (G : SimpleGraph (Fin 5)), G.CliqueFree 3 ∧ ∀ (H : SimpleGraph (Fin 5)),
    48→        H ≤ G → H.IsBipartite → 1 ≤ (G.edgeFinset \ H.edgeFinset).card := by
    49→  sorry
    50→
    51→/--
    52→The blow-up of the 5-cycle $C_5$: replace each vertex of $C_5$ with an independent set of $n$
    53→vertices, and connect two vertices iff their corresponding vertices in $C_5$ are adjacent.
    54→The vertex set is $\mathbb{Z}/5\mathbb{Z} \times \{0, \ldots, n-1\}$, where $(i, a)$ and $(j, b)$
    55→are adjacent iff $j = i + 1$ or $i = j + 1$ in $\mathbb{Z}/5\mathbb{Z}$.
    56→-/
    57→def blowupC5 (n : ℕ) : SimpleGraph (ZMod 5 × Fin n) :=
    58→  SimpleGraph.fromRel fun (i, _) (j, _) => i + 1 = j ∨ j + 1 = i
    59→
    60→/--
    61→The blow-up of $C_5$ shows that the bound $n^2$ in Erdős Problem 23 is tight:
    62→any bipartite subgraph must omit at least $n^2$ edges.
    63→-/
    64→@[category test, AMS 5]
    65→theorem blowupC5_tight (n : ℕ) (_hn : 0 < n) (H : SimpleGraph (ZMod 5 × Fin n))
    66→    (hH : H ≤ blowupC5 n) (hBip : H.IsBipartite) :
    67→    n ^ 2 ≤ ((blowupC5 n).edgeFinset \ H.edgeFinset).card := by
    68→  sorry
    69→
    70→/--
    71→Can every triangle-free graph on $5n$ vertices be made bipartite by deleting at most $n^2$ edges?
    72→-/
    73→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    74→theorem erdos_23 : answer(sorry) ↔
    75→    ∀ (n : ℕ) (V : Type) [Fintype V], Fintype.card V = 5 * n →
    76→      ∀ (G : SimpleGraph V), G.CliqueFree 3 →
    77→        ∃ (H : SimpleGraph V),
    78→          H ≤ G ∧ H.IsBipartite ∧ (G.edgeFinset \ H.edgeFinset).card ≤ n^2 := by
    79→  sorry
    80→
    81→-- TODO: add the remaining variants/statements/comments
    82→
    83→end Erdos23
    84→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 165
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/165](https://www.erdosproblems.com/165)
    23→
    24→Give an asymptotic formula for $R(3,k)$.
    25→
    26→$R(3,k)$ is the Ramsey number: the minimum $N$ such that every simple graph
    27→on $N$ vertices contains either a triangle ($3$-clique) or an independent set
    28→of size $k$ (equivalently, a $k$-clique in the complement).
    29→
    30→It is known that for some constant $c > 0$ and large $k$:
    31→$$
    32→  (c + o(1)) \frac{k^2}{\log k} \leq R(3,k) \leq (1 + o(1)) \frac{k^2}{\log k}
    33→$$
    34→
    35→The upper bound is due to Shearer [Sh83], improving Ajtai–Komlós–Szemerédi
    36→[AKS80]. The lower bound constant has been improved to $c \geq 1/2$ by
    37→Hefty–Horn–King–Pfender [HHKP25]. The conjectured asymptotic is
    38→$R(3,k) \sim \frac{1}{2} \frac{k^2}{\log k}$.
    39→
    40→[Sh83] Shearer, J. B., _A note on the independence number of triangle-free
    41→graphs_. Discrete Mathematics (1983).
    42→
    43→[AKS80] Ajtai, M., Komlós, J. and Szemerédi, E., _A note on Ramsey numbers_.
    44→Journal of Combinatorial Theory, Series A (1980).
    45→
    46→[HHKP25] Hefty, L., Horn, P., King, R. and Pfender, F., _On the Ramsey number
    47→$R(3,t)$_. (2025).
    48→
    49→[Er61] Erdős, P. (1961). [Er71] Erdős, P. (1971). [Er90b] Erdős, P. (1990).
    50→[Er93] Erdős, P. (1993). [Er97c] Erdős, P. (1997).
    51→-/
    52→
    53→open SimpleGraph Real
    54→
    55→namespace Erdos165
    56→
    57→/-- The Ramsey number $R(3,k)$: the minimum $N$ such that every simple graph
    58→on $N$ vertices contains either a triangle ($3$-clique) or an independent
    59→set of size $k$ (a $k$-clique in the complement). -/
    60→noncomputable def ramseyR3 (k : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    61→  sInf {N : ℕ | ∀ (G : SimpleGraph (Fin N)), ¬G.CliqueFree 3 ∨ ¬Gᶜ.CliqueFree k}
    62→
    63→/--
    64→Erdős Conjecture (Problem 165) [Er61, Er71, Er90b, Er93, Er97c]:
    65→
    66→There exists a constant $c > 0$ such that $R(3,k) \sim c \cdot k^2 / \log k$, i.e.,
    67→for all $\varepsilon > 0$ and all sufficiently large $k$:
    68→$$
    69→  (c - \varepsilon) \cdot \frac{k^2}{\log k} \leq R(3,k) \leq (c + \varepsilon) \cdot \frac{k^2}{\log k}.
    70→$$
    71→
    72→The conjectured value is $c = 1/2$.
    73→-/
    74→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    75→theorem erdos_165 : answer(sorry) ↔
    76→    ∃ c : ℝ, 0 < c ∧ ∀ ε : ℝ, 0 < ε →
    77→    ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ k : ℕ, N₀ ≤ k →
    78→      (c - ε) * ((k : ℝ) ^ 2 / Real.log (k : ℝ)) ≤ (ramseyR3 k : ℝ) ∧
    79→      (ramseyR3 k : ℝ) ≤ (c + ε) * ((k : ℝ) ^ 2 / Real.log (k : ℝ)) := by
    80→  sorry
    81→
    82→end Erdos165

    23→namespace SimpleGraph

    25→open Classical Finset List
    26→
    27→variable {α : Type*} [Fintype α] [DecidableEq α]
    28→
    29→/-- `Ls G` is the maximum number of leaves over all spanning trees of `G`.
    30→It is defined to be `0` when `G` is not connected. -/
    31→noncomputable def Ls (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℝ :=
    32→  letI spanningTrees := { T : Subgraph G | T.IsSpanning ∧ IsTree T.coe }
    33→  letI leaves (T : Subgraph G) := T.verts.toFinset.filter (fun v => T.degree v = 1)
    34→  letI num_leaves (T : Subgraph G) := (leaves T).card
    35→  sSup (Set.image (fun T => (num_leaves T : ℝ)) spanningTrees)
    36→
    37→/-- `n G` is the number of vertices of `G` as a real number. -/
    38→noncomputable def n (_ : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ := Fintype.card α
    39→
    40→/-- `m G` is the size of a maximum matching of `G`. -/
    41→noncomputable def m (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℝ :=
    42→  let matchings := { M : Subgraph G | M.IsMatching }
    43→  sSup (Set.image (fun M => (M.edgeSet.toFinset.card : ℝ)) matchings)
    44→
    45→/-- The maximum cardinality among all independent sets `s`
    46→    that maximize the quantity `|s| - |N(s)|`, where `N(s)`
    47→    is the neighborhood of the set `s`. -/
    48→noncomputable def aprime (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℝ :=
    49→  letI indep_sets : Finset (Finset α) := univ.powerset.filter (fun s => G.IsIndepSet (s : Set α))
    50→  letI diff (s : Finset α) : ℤ := (s.card : ℤ) - (⋃ v ∈ (s : Set α), G.neighborSet v).toFinset.card
    51→  letI max_diff := (indep_sets.image diff).max
    52→  letI critical_sets := indep_sets.filter (fun s ↦ diff s = max_diff.getD 0)
    53→  letI max_card := (critical_sets.image Finset.card).max
    54→  (max_card.getD 0 : ℝ)
    55→
    56→/-- `largestInducedForestSize G` is the size of a largest induced forest of `G`. -/
    57→noncomputable def largestInducedForestSize (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ :=
    58→  sSup { n | ∃ s : Finset α, (G.induce s).IsAcyclic ∧ s.card = n }
    59→
    60→/-- The degree sequence of a graph, sorted in nondecreasing order. -/
    61→noncomputable def degreeSequence (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : List ℕ :=
    62→  (Finset.univ.val.map fun v : α => G.degree v).sort (· ≤ ·)
    63→
    64→/--
    65→The maximum number of occurrences of any term of the degree sequence of `G`.
    66→-/
    67→noncomputable def degreeSequenceMultiplicity (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℕ :=
    68→  letI degs := degreeSequence G
    69→  (List.max? (degs.map (fun d => degs.count d))).getD 0
    70→
    71→/-- `largestInducedBipartiteSubgraphSize G` is the size of a largest induced
    72→bipartite subgraph of `G`. -/
    73→noncomputable def largestInducedBipartiteSubgraphSize (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ :=
    74→  sSup { n | ∃ s : Finset α, (G.induce s).IsBipartite ∧ s.card = n }
    75→
    76→/-- `b G` is the number of vertices of a largest induced bipartite subgraph of `G`.
    77→Returned as a real number. -/
    78→noncomputable def b (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ :=
    79→  (largestInducedBipartiteSubgraphSize G : ℝ)
    80→
    81→/-- Independence number of the neighbourhood of `v`. -/
    82→noncomputable def indepNeighborsCard (G : SimpleGraph α) (v : α) : ℕ :=
    83→  (G.induce (G.neighborSet v)).indepNum
    84→
    85→/-- The same quantity as a real number. -/
    86→noncomputable def indepNeighbors (G : SimpleGraph α) (v : α) : ℝ :=
    87→  (indepNeighborsCard G v : ℝ)
    88→
    89→/-- Average of `indepNeighbors` over all vertices. -/
    90→noncomputable def averageIndepNeighbors (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ :=
    91→  (∑ v ∈ Finset.univ, indepNeighbors G v) / (Fintype.card α : ℝ)
    92→
    93→/-- A unit distance graph in ℝ²:
    94→A graph where the vertices V are a collection of points in ℝ² and there is
    95→an edge between two points if and only if the distance between them is 1. -/
    96→def UnitDistancePlaneGraph (V : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : SimpleGraph V where
    97→  Adj x y := Dist.dist x y = 1
    98→  symm x y := by simp [PseudoMetricSpace.dist_comm]
    99→
   100→/--
   101→Two walks are internally disjoint if they share no vertices other than their endpoints.
   102→-/
   103→def InternallyDisjoint {V : Type*} {G : SimpleGraph V} {u v x y : V}
   104→    (p : G.Walk u v) (q : G.Walk x y) : Prop :=
   105→  Disjoint p.support.tail.dropLast q.support.tail.dropLast
   106→
   107→/--
   108→We say a graph is infinitely connected if any two vertices are connected by infinitely many
   109→pairwise disjoint paths.
   110→-/
   111→def InfinitelyConnected {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) : Prop :=
   112→  Pairwise fun u v ↦ ∃ P : Set (G.Walk u v),
   113→    P.Infinite ∧ (∀ p ∈ P, p.IsPath) ∧ P.Pairwise InternallyDisjoint
   114→
   115→/-- Infinite graphs: definitions for max degree and clique number so that the maximum
   116→degree (resp. clique number) of a graph with unbounded degree (resp. clique size) is
   117→`∞` rather than 0.
   118→-/
   119→noncomputable
   120→def edegree {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) (v : V) : ℕ∞ := (G.neighborSet v).encard
   121→
   122→noncomputable
   123→def emaxDegree {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) : ℕ∞ := ⨆ v, G.edegree v
   124→
   125→noncomputable
   126→def ecliqueNum {V : Type} (G : SimpleGraph V) : ℕ∞ := ⨆ (s : Finset V) (_ : G.IsClique s), #s
   127→
   128→end SimpleGraph

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 149
    21→
    22→The Erdős–Nešetřil conjecture states that the strong chromatic index of any graph with
    23→maximum degree Δ is at most 5Δ²/4.
    24→
    25→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/149](https://www.erdosproblems.com/149)
    26→
    27→[ErNe85] Erdős, P. and Nešetřil, J., proposed at a seminar in Prague (1985).
    28→-/
    29→
    30→open SimpleGraph
    31→
    32→namespace Erdos149
    33→
    34→/-- A set $S$ of edges of $G$ is strongly independent if for any two distinct edges
    35→$e_1, e_2 \in S$, every endpoint $u$ of $e_1$ and every endpoint $v$ of $e_2$ satisfy
    36→$u \neq v$ and $\neg G.\text{Adj}\; u\; v$. Equivalently, $S$ is an independent set in $L(G)^2$
    37→(the square of the line graph of $G$). -/
    38→def IsStronglyIndepEdgeSet {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V)
    39→    (S : Set (Sym2 V)) : Prop :=
    40→  S ⊆ G.edgeSet ∧
    41→  ∀ e₁ ∈ S, ∀ e₂ ∈ S, e₁ ≠ e₂ →
    42→    ∀ u ∈ e₁, ∀ v ∈ e₂, u ≠ v ∧ ¬G.Adj u v
    43→
    44→/-- The strong chromatic index $\chi'_s(G)$: the minimum number of strongly
    45→independent edge sets needed to partition the edges of $G$. Equivalently,
    46→this is the chromatic number of $L(G)^2$, the square of the line graph. -/
    47→noncomputable def strongChromaticIndex {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) : ℕ :=
    48→  sInf {k : ℕ | ∃ (c : G.edgeSet → Fin k),
    49→    ∀ (e₁ e₂ : G.edgeSet), c e₁ = c e₂ → e₁ ≠ e₂ →
    50→      ∀ u ∈ (e₁ : Sym2 V), ∀ v ∈ (e₂ : Sym2 V), u ≠ v ∧ ¬G.Adj u v}
    51→
    52→/-- Erdős–Nešetřil Strong Edge Coloring Conjecture (Erdős Problem 149) [ErNe85]:
    53→For any finite graph $G$ with maximum degree $\Delta$,
    54→$$
    55→  \chi'_s(G) \leq \frac{5}{4} \Delta^2,
    56→$$
    57→i.e. the edge set of $G$ can be partitioned into at most $\frac{5}{4}\Delta^2$ strongly
    58→independent edge sets. This bound is sharp: a blowup of $C_5$ with $\Delta = 2k$
    59→requires exactly $5k^2 = \frac{5}{4}\Delta^2$ strong colors. -/
    60→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    61→theorem erdos_149 :
    62→    ∀ (V : Type*) [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    63→      (G : SimpleGraph V) [DecidableRel G.Adj],
    64→      (strongChromaticIndex G : ℝ) ≤ (5 / 4 : ℝ) * (G.maxDegree : ℝ) ^ 2 := by
    65→  sorry
    66→
    67→end Erdos149

    19→/-!

    21→Authors: P. Erdős, S. Fajtlowicz and W. Staton,
    22→Published in Discrete Mathematics 92 (1991) 85–88.
    23→-/
    24→
    25→open BigOperators
    26→open Classical
    27→open scoped Finset
    28→
    29→/-- A sequence of natural numbers is **compact** on a set `S` if consecutive terms at distance
    30→`2` differ by `1` for all `k ∈ S`. -/
    31→def IsCompactSequenceOn (d : ℕ → ℕ) (S : Set ℕ) : Prop :=
    32→  ∀ k ∈ S, d (k + 2) = d k + 1
    33→
    34→namespace SimpleGraph
    35→
    36→variable {α : Type*} [Fintype α] [DecidableEq α]
    37→
    38→/-- The number of vertices of `G` having degree `d`. -/
    39→noncomputable def degreeFreq (G : SimpleGraph α) (d : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    40→  #{v | G.degree v = d}
    41→
    42→end SimpleGraph
    43→
    44→section lemmas
    45→
    46→variable (d : ℕ → ℕ) (n k r : ℕ)
    47→
    48→/-- **Lemma 1 (a)**
    49→If a sequence `d` is nondecreasing and no three terms are equal, then terms at distance 2 differ by at least 1. -/
    50→@[category API, AMS 5]
    51→lemma lemma1_a
    52→    (h_mono : Monotone d)
    53→    (h_no_three : ∀ k, d (k + 2) ≠ d k) :
    54→    1 ≤ d (k + 2) - d k := by
    55→  have : d k ≤ d (k + 2) := h_mono (by omega)
    56→  have := h_no_three k
    57→  omega
    58→
    59→/-- **Lemma 1 (b)**
    60→If a sequence `d` is nondecreasing and no three terms are equal, then terms at distance `2 * r` differ by at least `r`. -/
    61→@[category API, AMS 5]
    62→lemma lemma1_b
    63→    (h_mono : Monotone d)
    64→    (h_pos : ∀ k, 0 < d k)
    65→    (h_no_three : ∀ i, d (i + 2) ≠ d i) :
    66→    r ≤ d (k + 2 * r) - d k := by
    67→  sorry
    68→
    69→/-- **Lemma 2 (a)**
    70→Inequality involving sums of terms of a nondecreasing sequence with no three terms equal. -/
    71→@[category API, AMS 5]
    72→lemma lemma2_a
    73→    (h_mono : Monotone d)
    74→    (h_pos : ∀ k, 0 < d k)
    75→    (h_no_three : ∀ i, d (i + 2) ≠ d i) :
    76→    2 * n * n ≤
    77→      ∑ i ∈ .Icc (2 * n + 1) (4 * n), d i -
    78→        ∑ i ∈ .Icc 1 (2 * n), d i := by
    79→  sorry
    80→
    81→/-- **Lemma 2 (b)**
    82→Inequality involving sums of terms of a nondecreasing sequence with no three terms equal. -/
    83→@[category API, AMS 5]
    84→lemma lemma2_b
    85→    (h_mono : Monotone d)
    86→    (h_pos : ∀ k, 0 < d k)
    87→    (h_no_three : ∀ i, d (i + 2) ≠ d i) :
    88→    2 * n * n + 2 * n + 1 ≤
    89→      ∑ i ∈ .Icc (2 * n + 1) (4 * n + 1), d i -
    90→        ∑ i ∈ .Icc 1 (2 * n), d i := by
    91→  sorry
    92→
    93→/-- **Lemma 2 (c)**
    94→Inequality involving sums of terms of a nondecreasing sequence with no three terms equal. -/
    95→@[category API, AMS 5]
    96→lemma lemma2_c
    97→    (h_mono : Monotone d)
    98→    (h_pos : ∀ k, 0 < d k)
    99→    (h_no_three : ∀ i, d (i + 2) ≠ d i) :
   100→    2 * n * n + 2 * n ≤
   101→      (∑ i ∈ .Icc (2 * n + 2) (4 * n + 2), d i) -
   102→        ∑ i ∈ .Icc 1 (2 * n + 1), d i := by
   103→  sorry
   104→
   105→/-- **Lemma 2 (d)**
   106→Inequality involving sums of terms of a nondecreasing sequence with no three terms equal. -/
   107→@[category API, AMS 5]
   108→lemma lemma2_d
   109→    (h_mono : Monotone d)
   110→    (h_pos : ∀ k, 0 < d k)
   111→    (h_no_three : ∀ i, d (i + 2) ≠ d i) :
   112→    2 * n * n + 4 * n + 2 ≤
   113→      (∑ i ∈ .Icc (2 * n + 2) (4 * n + 3), d i) -
   114→        ∑ i ∈ .Icc 1 (2 * n + 1), d i := by
   115→  sorry
   116→
   117→end lemmas
   118→
   119→namespace SimpleGraph
   120→
   121→variable {α : Type*} [Fintype α] [DecidableEq α]
   122→
   123→
   124→/-- The degree sequence of `G` is **compact** if it satisfies
   125→`IsCompactSequenceOn` for all valid indices `k` such that `k + 2 < Fintype.card α`. -/
   126→def HasCompactdegreeSequence (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : Prop :=
   127→  IsCompactSequenceOn (fun k => (degreeSequence G).getD k 0) {k | k + 2 < Fintype.card α}
   128→
   129→/-- **Theorem 1.** If a triangle-free graph has `f = 2`,
   130→then it is bipartite, has minimum degree `1`, and
   131→its degree sequence is compact. -/
   132→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
   133→theorem theorem1 (G : SimpleGraph α) (h_conn: G.Connected) [DecidableRel G.Adj]
   134→    (h₁ : G.CliqueFree 3) (h₂ : degreeSequenceMultiplicity G = 2) :
   135→    G.IsBipartite ∧ G.minDegree = 1 ∧ HasCompactdegreeSequence G := by
   136→  sorry
   137→
   138→/-- **Lemma 3.** For every `n` there exists a bipartite graph with
   139→`8 n` vertices, minimum degree `n + 1`, and `f = 3`. -/
   140→@[category API, AMS 5]
   141→lemma lemma3 (n : ℕ) (hn : 0 < n) :
   142→    ∃ (G : SimpleGraph (Fin (8 * n))) (_ : DecidableRel G.Adj),
   143→      G.IsBipartite ∧ G.minDegree = n + 1 ∧ degreeSequenceMultiplicity G = 3 := by
   144→  sorry
   145→
   146→/-- **Lemma 4.** Let `G` be a triangle-free graph with `n` vertices and let `v` be a vertex of `G`.
   147→There exists a triangle-free graph `H` containing `G` as an induced subgraph such that:
   148→(i) the degree of `v` in `H` is one more than its degree in `G`;
   149→(ii) for every vertex `w` of `G` other than `v` the degree of `w` in `H` is the same as its degree in `G`;
   150→(iii) if `J` is the subgraph of `H` induced by the vertices not in `G`, then `f(J)=3` and `δ(J) ≥ 2n`. -/
   151→@[category API, AMS 5]
   152→lemma lemma4 (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] (h_conn: G.Connected)
   153→    (h₁ : G.CliqueFree 3) (v : α) :
   154→    ∃ (β : Type*) (_ : Fintype β) (H : SimpleGraph β) (_ : DecidableRel H.Adj) (i : G ↪g H),
   155→      H.CliqueFree 3 ∧
   156→      H.degree (i v) = G.degree v + 1 ∧
   157→      (∀ w ≠ v, H.degree (i w) = G.degree w) ∧
   158→      let J := H.induce (Set.compl (Set.range i))
   159→      degreeSequenceMultiplicity J = 3 ∧ J.minDegree ≥ 2 * Fintype.card α := by
   160→  sorry
   161→
   162→/-- **Theorem 2.** Every triangle-free graph is an induced subgraph of one
   163→with `f = 3`. -/
   164→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
   165→theorem theorem2 (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] (h_conn: G.Connected)
   166→    (h : G.CliqueFree 3) :
   167→    ∃ (β : Type*) (_ : Fintype β) (H : SimpleGraph β) (_ : DecidableRel H.Adj) (i : G ↪g H),
   168→      H.CliqueFree 3 ∧ degreeSequenceMultiplicity H = 3 := by
   169→  sorry
   170→
   171→/-- `F n` is the smallest number of vertices of a triangle-free graph
   172→with chromatic number `n` and `f = 3`. -/
   173→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
   174→noncomputable def F (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
   175→  sInf { p | ∃ (G : SimpleGraph (Fin p)) (_ : DecidableRel G.Adj),
   176→    G.CliqueFree 3 ∧ G.chromaticNumber = n ∧ degreeSequenceMultiplicity G = 3 }
   177→
   178→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
   179→theorem F_three : F 3 = 7 := by
   180→  sorry
   181→
   182→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
   183→theorem F_four_le : F 4 ≤ 19 := by
   184→  sorry
   185→
   186→end SimpleGraph

    23→noncomputable def Matrix.IsHermitian.maxEigenvalue {𝕜 : Type*} [Field 𝕜] [RCLike 𝕜]

    27→namespace SimpleGraph

    29→open Classical

    31→variable {α : Type*} [Fintype α] [DecidableEq α]
    32→
    33→/-- Abbreviation for the average independence number of the neighborhoods.
    34→-/
    35→noncomputable abbrev l (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ := averageIndepNeighbors G
    36→
    37→/-- The same quantity under a different name, used in some conjectures.
    38→-/
    39→noncomputable abbrev l_avg (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ := averageIndepNeighbors G
    40→
    41→/-- Independent domination number of `G`. -/
    42→noncomputable def gi (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ := G.indepDominationNumber
    43→
    44→/-- `temp_v G v = deg(v)/(n(G) - deg(v))`. -/
    45→noncomputable def temp_v (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] (v : α) : ℝ :=
    46→  let n := Fintype.card α
    47→  let deg := G.degree v
    48→  if n = deg then 0 else (deg : ℝ) / ((n : ℝ) - (deg : ℝ))
    49→
    50→/-- Maximum of `temp_v` over all vertices. -/
    51→noncomputable def MaxTemp (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] [Fintype α] [Nonempty α] : ℝ :=
    52→  let temps := Finset.univ.image (temp_v G)
    53→  temps.max' (Finset.image_nonempty.mpr Finset.univ_nonempty)
    54→
    55→/-- Cardinality of the union of the neighbourhoods of the ends of the non-edge `e`. -/
    56→def non_edge_neighborhood_card (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] (e : Sym2 α) : ℕ :=
    57→  Sym2.lift ⟨fun u v => (G.neighborFinset u ∪ G.neighborFinset v).card,
    58→    fun u v => by simp [Finset.union_comm]⟩ e
    59→
    60→/-- Minimum size of the neighbourhood of a non-edge of `G`. -/
    61→noncomputable def NG (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℝ :=
    62→  let non_edges := (compl G).edgeFinset
    63→  if h : non_edges.Nonempty then
    64→    let neighbor_sizes := non_edges.image (non_edge_neighborhood_card G)
    65→    (neighbor_sizes.min' (Finset.Nonempty.image h _))
    66→  else
    67→    (Fintype.card α : ℝ)
    68→
    69→/-- Size of a largest induced forest of `G` expressed as a real number. -/
    70→noncomputable def S (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ :=
    71→  let card := Fintype.card α
    72→  if card < 2 then 0 else
    73→    let degrees := Multiset.ofList (List.map (fun v => G.degree v) Finset.univ.toList)
    74→    let sorted_degrees := degrees.sort (· ≤ ·)
    75→    ↑((sorted_degrees[card - 2]?).getD 0)
    76→
    77→/-- Local eccentricity of a vertex. -/
    78→noncomputable def local_eccentricity (G : SimpleGraph α) (v : α) : ENat :=
    79→  sSup (Set.range (G.edist v))
    80→
    81→/-- The largest integer less than or equal to `x`. -/
    82→noncomputable def FLOOR (x : ℝ) : ℝ := ⌊x⌋
    83→
    84→/-- Eccentricity of a vertex. -/
    85→noncomputable def eccentricity (G : SimpleGraph α) (v : α) : ℕ∞ :=

    87→
    88→/-- The minimum eccentricity among all vertices. -/
    89→noncomputable def minEccentricity (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ∞ :=

    91→
    92→/-- The set of vertices of minimum eccentricity. -/

    94→  {v : α | eccentricity G v = minEccentricity G}
    95→
    96→/-- The maximum eccentricity among all vertices. -/
    97→noncomputable def maxEccentricity (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ∞ :=
    98→  sSup (Set.range (eccentricity G))
    99→
   100→/-- The set of vertices of maximum eccentricity. -/
   101→noncomputable def maxEccentricityVertices (G : SimpleGraph α) : Set α :=
   102→  {v : α | eccentricity G v = maxEccentricity G}
   103→
   104→/-- Distance from a vertex to a finite set. -/
   105→noncomputable def distToSet (G : SimpleGraph α) (v : α) (S : Set α) : ℕ :=
   106→  if h : S.toFinset.Nonempty then
   107→    (S.toFinset.image (fun s => G.dist v s)).min' (Finset.Nonempty.image h _)
   108→  else 0
   109→
   110→/-- Average distance of `G`. -/
   111→noncomputable def averageDistance (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ :=
   112→  if Fintype.card α > 1 then
   113→    (∑ u ∈ Finset.univ, ∑ v ∈ Finset.univ, (G.dist u v : ℝ)) /
   114→      ((Fintype.card α : ℝ) * ((Fintype.card α : ℝ) - 1))
   115→  else
   116→    0
   117→
   118→/-- The floor of the average distance of `G`. -/
   119→noncomputable def path (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ :=
   120→  if G.Connected then
   121→    Nat.floor (averageDistance G)
   122→  else
   123→    0
   124→
   125→/-- Auxiliary quantity `ecc` used in conjecture 34. -/
   126→noncomputable def ecc (G : SimpleGraph α) (S : Set α) : ℕ :=
   127→  let s_comp := Finset.univ.filter (fun v => v ∉ S)
   128→  if h : s_comp.Nonempty then
   129→    (s_comp.image (fun v => distToSet G v S)).max' (Finset.Nonempty.image h _)
   130→  else 0
   131→
   132→/-- Average distance from all vertices to a given set. -/
   133→noncomputable def distavg (G : SimpleGraph α) (S : Set α) : ℝ :=
   134→  if Fintype.card α > 0 then
   135→    (∑ v ∈ Finset.univ, (distToSet G v S : ℝ)) / (Fintype.card α : ℝ)
   136→  else
   137→    0
   138→
   139→/-- A family of paths covering all vertices without overlaps. -/
   140→def IsPathCover (G : SimpleGraph α) (P : Finset (Finset α)) : Prop :=
   141→  (∀ s1 ∈ P, ∀ s2 ∈ P, s1 ≠ s2 → Disjoint s1 s2) ∧
   142→  (Finset.univ ⊆ P.biUnion id) ∧
   143→  (∀ s ∈ P, ∃ (u v : α) (p : G.Walk u v), p.IsPath ∧ s = p.support.toFinset)
   144→
   145→/-- Minimum size of a path cover of `G`. -/
   146→noncomputable def pathCoverNumber (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ :=
   147→  sInf { k | ∃ P : Finset (Finset α), P.card = k ∧ IsPathCover G P }
   148→
   149→/-- The same quantity as a real number. -/
   150→noncomputable def p (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ := (pathCoverNumber G : ℝ)
   151→
   152→/-- The Wiener index of `G`, which is the sum of distances between all
   153→pairs of vertices. -/
   154→noncomputable def wienerIndex (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ :=
   155→  ∑ uv : Sym2 α, uv.lift ⟨fun u v ↦ G.dist u v, by simp [dist_comm]⟩
   156→
   157→/-- Auxiliary function for Szeged index: counts vertices closer to u than v. -/
   158→noncomputable def szeged_aux (G : SimpleGraph α) (u v : α) : ℕ :=
   159→  (Finset.univ.filter (fun w => G.edist w u < G.edist w v)).card
   160→
   161→/-- The Szeged index of `G`.
   162→
   163→This is define as the sum `∑_{uv ∈ E(G)} n_u(u,v) * n_v(u,v)` where
   164→`n_u(uv)` is the number of vertices closer to `u` than `v`.
   165→-/
   166→noncomputable def szegedIndex (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℕ :=
   167→  ∑ e ∈ G.edgeFinset,
   168→    e.lift ⟨fun u v => szeged_aux G u v * szeged_aux G v u, by simp [mul_comm]⟩
   169→
   170→/-- The average degree of `G`. -/
   171→noncomputable def averageDegree (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℚ  :=
   172→  (∑ v, (G.degree v : ℚ)) / (Fintype.card α : ℚ)
   173→
   174→/-- The multiset of degrees of a graph. -/
   175→def degreeMultiset (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : Multiset ℕ :=
   176→  Finset.univ.val.map fun v => G.degree v
   177→
   178→/-- The annihilation number of a graph. This is the largest number of degrees that can be added
   179→together without going over the total number of edges of that graph. -/
   180→def annihilationNumber (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℕ :=
   181→  -- Calculate the limit: The number of edges (Sum of degrees / 2)
   182→  letI limit := G.edgeFinset.card
   183→
   184→  -- The set of all multisets of degrees that sum to less than or equal to `limit`
   185→  Finset.Iic (degreeMultiset G)
   186→    |>.filter (fun S ↦ Multiset.sum S ≤ limit)
   187→    |>.sup Multiset.card
   188→
   189→/--
   190→Computes the annihilation number of a graph G.
   191→
   192→It calculates the degree sequence, sorts it ascendingly, and finds the largest prefix length 'k'
   193→(where `0 ≤ k ≤ |V(G)|`) such that the sum of the prefix is less than or equal to the sum of the corresponding suffix.
   194→-/
   195→noncomputable def annihilationNumber' (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℕ :=
   196→  -- 1. Get the degree sequence sorted in ascending order.
   197→  -- G.degree_list returns the list of degrees.
   198→  letI degrees := (Finset.univ.image fun v => G.degree v).sort (· ≤ ·)
   199→
   200→  -- 2. Define the condition for the annihilation number.
   201→  -- k represents the number of smallest degrees considered (the length of the prefix).
   202→  letI condition (k : ℕ) : Bool := (degrees.take k).sum ≤ (degrees.drop k).sum
   203→
   204→  -- 3. Find the maximum k in {0, ..., n} satisfying the condition.
   205→  -- List.range (n + 1) generates the list [0, 1, ..., n].
   206→  letI candidates := (List.range (Fintype.card α + 1)).filter condition
   207→
   208→  -- 4. Return the maximum candidate.
   209→  -- The list of candidates is guaranteed to be non-empty because k=0 always satisfies
   210→  -- the condition (0 ≤ sum of all degrees).
   211→  candidates.getLast!
   212→
   213→set_option linter.unusedSectionVars false in
   214→-- TODO(Paul-Lez): debug the issue with the unused variable linter...
   215→proof_wanted annihilationNumberEq (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] :
   216→    annihilationNumber G = annihilationNumber' G
   217→
   218→/-!
   219→## Residue
   220→The residue of a graph is the number of zeros remaining after iteratively applying the Havel-Hakimi
   221→algorithm to the degree sequence until all remaining degrees are zero.
   222→-/
   223→
   224→/--
   225→Helper function: Performs one step of the Havel-Hakimi reduction on a degree sequence.
   226→Assumes the input list `s` is sorted descending.
   227→Removes the first element `d`, decrements the next `d` elements by 1, and re-sorts the list descending.
   228→
   229→Note: when `s` is the list of vertices arising from a simple graph, if the first index is `s` then
   230→the degree list always has length at least `s+1` so this makes sense.
   231→-/
   232→private def havelHakimiStep (s : List ℕ) : List ℕ :=
   233→  match s with
   234→  | [] => []
   235→  | d :: rest =>
   236→    -- Split the rest into the part to decrement (first d elements) and the remaining part.
   237→    let (to_decrement, remaining) := rest.splitAt d
   238→    -- Decrement the elements
   239→    let decremented := to_decrement.map (· - 1)
   240→    -- Combine and re-sort descending.
   241→    (decremented ++ remaining).mergeSort (· ≥ ·)
   242→
   243→/--
   244→Auxiliary function to calculate the residue recursively.
   245→Applies Havel-Hakimi steps until the sequence consists only of zeros or is empty.
   246→-/
   247→private partial def residueAux : List ℕ → ℕ
   248→  | [] => 0        -- Empty sequence, residue is 0.
   249→  | 0 :: s => 1 + s.length -- If the largest degree is 0 (and the list is sorted), all are 0.
   250→  | s => residueAux (havelHakimiStep s) -- Apply one reduction step and recurse.
   251→
   252→/--
   253→Computes the residue of a graph G, ,i.e. the number of zeros remaining after iteratively applying the Havel-Hakimi
   254→algorithm to the degree sequence until all remaining degrees are zero.
   255→Starts with the descending degree sequence and applies the Havel-Hakimi process.
   256→-/
   257→noncomputable def residue (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℕ :=
   258→  -- Get the degree sequence sorted in descending order and apply `residueAux`.
   259→  residueAux ((Finset.univ.image fun v => G.degree v).sort (· ≥ ·))
   260→
   261→/--
   262→Fractional alpha. This is defined as
   263→$$
   264→\max_x \sum_{v \in V} x_v
   265→$$
   266→subject to $0 \le x_v \le 1$ for all $v \in V$ and
   267→$x_u + x_v \le 1$ whenever $(u, v)$ are connected by an edge.
   268→-/
   269→noncomputable def fractionalAlpha (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℝ :=
   270→  sSup {(∑ i, x i) | (x : α → ℝ) (_ : ∀ v, x v ∈ Set.Icc 0 1)
   271→    (_ : ∀ u v, G.Adj u v → x u + x v ≤ 1)}
   272→
   273→/--
   274→Lovász Theta Function (ϑ(G))
   275→The Lovász theta function is defined as:
   276→ϑ(G) = min λ_max(A)
   277→where the minimum is taken over all symmetric matrices A such that:
   278→
   279→A_ij = 1 for all i = j (diagonal entries are 1)
   280→A_ij = 0 for all {i,j} ∈ E (entries corresponding to edges are 0)
   281→A is positive semidefinite
   282→
   283→Here λ_max(A) denotes the maximum eigenvalue of A.
   284→-/
   285→noncomputable def lovaczThetaFunction
   286→    (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℝ :=
   287→  sInf {(Matrix.IsHermitian.maxEigenvalue hA) | (A : Matrix α α ℝ) (hA : A.IsHermitian)
   288→      (_ : ∀ i, A i i = 1) (_ : ∀ i j, G.Adj i j → A i j = 0)}
   289→
   290→/--
   291→Given a simple graph `G` with adjacency matrix `A`, this is the number `n₀ + min n₊ n₋` where:
   292→- `n₀` is the multiplicity of zero as an eigenvalue of `A`
   293→- `n₊` is the number of positive eigenvalues of `A` (counting multiplicities)
   294→- `n₋` is the number of negative eigenvalues of `A` (counting multiplicities)
   295→-/
   296→noncomputable def cvetkovic (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℕ :=
   297→  letI A : Matrix α α ℝ := G.adjMatrix ℝ
   298→  letI spectrum : Multiset ℝ := (Matrix.charpoly A).roots
   299→  letI positive_count : ℕ := spectrum.countP (fun x => 0 < x)
   300→  letI negative_count : ℕ := spectrum.countP (fun x => 0 > x)
   301→  letI zero_count : ℕ := spectrum.countP (fun x => 0 = x)
   302→  zero_count + min positive_count negative_count
   303→
   304→end SimpleGraph
   305→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 106
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/106](https://www.erdosproblems.com/106)
    23→
    24→Draw $n$ non-overlapping squares (possibly rotated) inside the unit square. Let $f(n)$ be
    25→the maximum total side length. Is $f(k^2 + 1) = k$ for every positive integer $k$?
    26→-/
    27→
    28→open BigOperators Real
    29→
    30→namespace Erdos106
    31→
    32→/--
    33→A square placement in $\mathbb{R}^2$: a center point, a positive side length, and a
    34→rotation angle (in radians) measuring how far the square is rotated from
    35→the standard axis-aligned orientation.
    36→-/
    37→structure SquarePlacement where
    38→  center : ℝ × ℝ
    39→  side   : ℝ
    40→  angle  : ℝ
    41→  side_pos : 0 < side
    42→
    43→/--
    44→The closed region occupied by a square placement. A point $p$ lies in the
    45→region iff, when translated so the center is at the origin and then rotated
    46→by $-\mathrm{angle}$, its coordinates both lie in $[-\mathrm{side}/2, \mathrm{side}/2]$.
    47→-/
    48→noncomputable def SquarePlacement.region (sq : SquarePlacement) : Set (ℝ × ℝ) :=
    49→  {p : ℝ × ℝ |
    50→    let u :=  (p.1 - sq.center.1) * cos sq.angle + (p.2 - sq.center.2) * sin sq.angle
    51→    let v := -(p.1 - sq.center.1) * sin sq.angle + (p.2 - sq.center.2) * cos sq.angle
    52→    |u| ≤ sq.side / 2 ∧ |v| ≤ sq.side / 2}
    53→
    54→/--
    55→The open interior of a square placement (strict inequalities).
    56→-/
    57→noncomputable def SquarePlacement.sqInterior (sq : SquarePlacement) : Set (ℝ × ℝ) :=
    58→  {p : ℝ × ℝ |
    59→    let u :=  (p.1 - sq.center.1) * cos sq.angle + (p.2 - sq.center.2) * sin sq.angle
    60→    let v := -(p.1 - sq.center.1) * sin sq.angle + (p.2 - sq.center.2) * cos sq.angle
    61→    |u| < sq.side / 2 ∧ |v| < sq.side / 2}
    62→
    63→/--
    64→The unit square $[0,1]^2 \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$.
    65→-/
    66→def unitSquare : Set (ℝ × ℝ) :=
    67→  {p : ℝ × ℝ | 0 ≤ p.1 ∧ p.1 ≤ 1 ∧ 0 ≤ p.2 ∧ p.2 ≤ 1}
    68→
    69→/--
    70→A valid configuration of $n$ squares inside the unit square:
    71→1. Each square's closed region is contained in the unit square.
    72→2. No two distinct squares share an interior point (their open interiors
    73→   are disjoint).
    74→-/
    75→def IsValidSquareConfig (n : ℕ) (config : Fin n → SquarePlacement) : Prop :=
    76→  (∀ i : Fin n, (config i).region ⊆ unitSquare) ∧
    77→  (∀ i j : Fin n, i ≠ j → Disjoint (config i).sqInterior (config j).sqInterior)
    78→
    79→/--
    80→$f(n)$ is the supremum of the total side-length sum over all valid configurations
    81→of $n$ (possibly rotated) squares inside the unit square with pairwise disjoint
    82→interiors.
    83→-/
    84→noncomputable def f (n : ℕ) : ℝ :=
    85→  sSup {s : ℝ | ∃ config : Fin n → SquarePlacement,
    86→    IsValidSquareConfig n config ∧ s = ∑ i : Fin n, (config i).side}
    87→
    88→/--
    89→Erdős Problem 106:
    90→
    91→Draw $n$ squares (not necessarily axis-aligned) inside the unit square $[0,1]^2$
    92→with no two squares sharing a common interior point. Let $f(n)$ be the maximum
    93→possible sum of the side-lengths of the squares.
    94→
    95→**Conjecture**: $f(k^2 + 1) = k$ for every positive integer $k$.
    96→
    97→Background:
    98→- It follows easily from the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality that $f(k^2) = k$.
    99→- The lower bound $f(k^2 + 1) \geq k$ is elementary: subdivide $[0,1]^2$ into $k^2$
   100→  squares of side $1/k$, then replace any one of them by two squares of side
   101→  $1/(2k)$; the total side-length is $(k^2 - 1)/k + 2 \cdot 1/(2k) = k$.
   102→- The conjecture asserts that this lower bound is tight: no configuration of
   103→  $k^2 + 1$ squares can exceed the total side-length $k$ achievable with $k^2$ squares.
   104→- Baek, Koizumi, and Ueoro (2024) proved the axis-aligned variant: if all
   105→  squares are required to have sides parallel to the coordinate axes, then the
   106→  supremum equals $k$.
   107→-/
   108→@[category research open, AMS 52]
   109→theorem erdos_106 :
   110→    answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ k : ℕ, 0 < k → f (k ^ 2 + 1) = (k : ℝ) := by
   111→  sorry
   112→
   113→end Erdos106
   114→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 130
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/130](https://www.erdosproblems.com/130)
    23→
    24→Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ be an infinite set with no three collinear and no four concyclic
    25→points. Can the integer-distance graph on $A$ (adjacent iff at integer distance) have infinite
    26→chromatic number?
    27→
    28→[Er97b] Erdős, P., _Some of my favourite problems which recently have been solved_,
    29→Proceedings of the International Conference on Discrete Mathematics (ICDM) (1997).
    30→
    31→[AnEr45] Anning, N.H. and Erdős, P., _Integral distances_, Bull. Amer. Math. Soc. (1945),
    32→598-600.
    33→-/
    34→
    35→open SimpleGraph
    36→
    37→namespace Erdos130
    38→
    39→/-- A set of four points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ is concyclic if they all lie on a common circle
    40→(i.e., there exist a center $O \in \mathbb{R}^2$ and radius $r > 0$ such that each of the
    41→four points is at distance $r$ from $O$). -/
    42→def Concyclic (a b c d : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) : Prop :=
    43→  ∃ (center : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) (radius : ℝ),
    44→    0 < radius ∧
    45→    dist a center = radius ∧
    46→    dist b center = radius ∧
    47→    dist c center = radius ∧
    48→    dist d center = radius
    49→
    50→/-- A point set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ is in Erdős general position if no three distinct
    51→points of $A$ are collinear, and no four distinct points of $A$ are concyclic. -/
    52→def ErdosGeneralPosition (A : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    53→  (∀ p q r : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2),
    54→    p ∈ A → q ∈ A → r ∈ A →
    55→    p ≠ q → p ≠ r → q ≠ r →
    56→    ¬Collinear ℝ ({p, q, r} : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)))) ∧
    57→  (∀ p q r s : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2),
    58→    p ∈ A → q ∈ A → r ∈ A → s ∈ A →
    59→    p ≠ q → p ≠ r → p ≠ s → q ≠ r → q ≠ s → r ≠ s →
    60→    ¬Concyclic p q r s)
    61→
    62→/-- The integer-distance graph on a point set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$: vertices are points
    63→of $A$, and two distinct points are adjacent if and only if their Euclidean distance
    64→is a positive integer. -/
    65→noncomputable def intDistGraph (A : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) :
    66→    SimpleGraph ↥A where
    67→  Adj p q := (p : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) ≠ q ∧
    68→    ∃ n : ℕ, 0 < n ∧ dist (p : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) (q : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) = n
    69→  symm := fun _p _q ⟨hne, n, hn, hd⟩ =>
    70→    ⟨hne.symm, n, hn, by rw [_root_.dist_comm]; exact hd⟩
    71→  loopless := fun _ ⟨hne, _⟩ => hne rfl
    72→
    73→/--
    74→Erdős Problem 130 [Er97b] (asked by Andrásfai and Erdős):
    75→Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ be an infinite set with no three points collinear and no four
    76→points concyclic. Consider the integer-distance graph $G$ on $A$, where two distinct points
    77→are adjacent if and only if their Euclidean distance is a positive integer.
    78→
    79→Can the chromatic number of $G$ be infinite?
    80→
    81→Note: The clique number is always finite. The Anning–Erdős theorem [AnEr45]
    82→shows that any infinite set of points in the plane with all pairwise integer
    83→distances must be collinear; since $A$ has no three collinear points, $A$ contains
    84→no infinite complete subgraph.
    85→-/
    86→@[category research open, AMS 5 51]
    87→theorem erdos_130 : answer(sorry) ↔
    88→    ∃ (A : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))),
    89→      A.Infinite ∧ ErdosGeneralPosition A ∧
    90→      (intDistGraph A).chromaticNumber = ⊤ := by
    91→  sorry
    92→
    93→end Erdos130
    94→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 114
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/114](https://www.erdosproblems.com/114)
    23→
    24→Erdős–Herzog–Piranian conjecture: the arc length of the level curve
    25→$\{z \in \mathbb{C} : |p(z)| = 1\}$ of a monic polynomial of degree $n$ is maximized when
    26→$p(z) = z^n - 1$.
    27→
    28→[EHP58] Erdős, P., Herzog, F. and Piranian, G., _Metric properties of polynomials_,
    29→J. Analyse Math. 6 (1958), 125–148.
    30→-/
    31→
    32→open scoped ENNReal
    33→
    34→open Polynomial MeasureTheory
    35→
    36→namespace Erdos114
    37→
    38→/-- The unit level curve of a complex polynomial $p$: the set of $z \in \mathbb{C}$ with
    39→$|p(z)| = 1$. -/
    40→def levelCurveUnit (p : Polynomial ℂ) : Set ℂ :=
    41→  {z : ℂ | ‖p.eval z‖ = 1}
    42→
    43→/-- The arc length of a subset of $\mathbb{C}$, given by the 1-dimensional Hausdorff measure. -/
    44→noncomputable def arcLength (S : Set ℂ) : ℝ≥0∞ :=
    45→  Measure.hausdorffMeasure 1 S
    46→
    47→/--
    48→Erdős–Herzog–Piranian Conjecture (Problem #114) [EHP58]:
    49→If $p(z) \in \mathbb{C}[z]$ is a monic polynomial of degree $n \geq 1$, then the length of the
    50→curve $\{z \in \mathbb{C} : |p(z)| = 1\}$ is maximized when $p(z) = z^n - 1$.
    51→
    52→That is, for every monic polynomial $p$ of degree $n$,
    53→$$\mathrm{length}(\{z : |p(z)| = 1\}) \leq \mathrm{length}(\{z : |z^n - 1| = 1\}).$$
    54→
    55→Known partial results:
    56→- The curve for $p(z) = z^n - 1$ has length $2n + O(1)$, so the conjecture implies
    57→  the maximal length $f(n)$ satisfies $f(n) = 2n + O(1)$.
    58→- Dolzhenko (1961): $f(n) \leq 4\pi n$.
    59→- Borwein (1995): $f(n) \ll n$.
    60→- Eremenko–Hayman (1999): $f(n) \leq 9.173n$; full conjecture holds for $n = 2$.
    61→- Danchenko (2007): $f(n) \leq 2\pi n$.
    62→- Fryntov–Nazarov (2008): $f(n) \leq 2n + O(n^{7/8})$; $z^n - 1$ is a local maximiser.
    63→- Tao (2025): $z^n - 1$ is the unique maximiser (up to rotation) for all large $n$.
    64→-/
    65→@[category research open, AMS 30]
    66→theorem erdos_114 :
    67→    ∀ n : ℕ, 1 ≤ n →
    68→    ∀ p : Polynomial ℂ, p.Monic → p.natDegree = n →
    69→      arcLength (levelCurveUnit p) ≤
    70→      arcLength (levelCurveUnit ((X : Polynomial ℂ) ^ n - 1)) := by
    71→  sorry
    72→
    73→end Erdos114
    74→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1028
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1028](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1028)
    23→
    24→Let $H(n) = \min_f \max_{X \subseteq \{1,\ldots,n\}} \left|\sum_{x \neq y \in X} f(x,y)\right|$,
    25→where $f$ ranges over all functions $f\colon \{1,\ldots,n\}^2 \to \{-1,1\}$. Estimate $H(n)$.
    26→
    27→Erdős [Er63d] proved $n/4 \le H(n) \ll n^{3/2}$.
    28→Erdős and Spencer [ErSp71] proved that $H(n) \gg n^{3/2}$.
    29→
    30→Together these give $H(n) = \Theta(n^{3/2})$.
    31→-/
    32→
    33→open Finset
    34→
    35→namespace Erdos1028
    36→
    37→/-- The discrepancy sum of a $\pm 1$ function $f$ over a subset $X$ of $\operatorname{Fin}(n)$:
    38→$\sum_{x \neq y \in X} f(x, y)$ over ordered pairs with $x \neq y$. -/
    39→def discrepancySum (n : ℕ) (f : Fin n → Fin n → ℤ) (X : Finset (Fin n)) : ℤ :=
    40→  X.sum fun x => (X.filter (· ≠ x)).sum fun y => f x y
    41→
    42→/--
    43→Erdős Problem 1028 [Er63d][ErSp71]:
    44→
    45→$H(n) = \Theta(n^{3/2})$, where
    46→$H(n) = \min_f \max_{X \subseteq \{1,\ldots,n\}} \left|\sum_{x \neq y \in X} f(x,y)\right|$
    47→and $f$ ranges over all $\pm 1$ valued functions on pairs.
    48→
    49→This is equivalent to two bounds:
    50→- Lower bound (Erdős–Spencer [ErSp71]): every $\pm 1$ function $f$ has some subset $X$
    51→  with discrepancy at least $C_1 \cdot n^{3/2}$.
    52→- Upper bound (Erdős [Er63d]): there exists a $\pm 1$ function $f$ such that all subsets
    53→  have discrepancy at most $C_2 \cdot n^{3/2}$.
    54→-/
    55→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    56→theorem erdos_1028 :
    57→    ∃ C₁ C₂ : ℝ, C₁ > 0 ∧ C₂ > 0 ∧
    58→    ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N₀ →
    59→    -- Lower bound: every ±1 function has a subset with large discrepancy
    60→    (∀ f : Fin n → Fin n → ℤ, (∀ i j, f i j = 1 ∨ f i j = -1) →
    61→      ∃ X : Finset (Fin n),
    62→        C₁ * (n : ℝ) ^ ((3 : ℝ) / 2) ≤ |(discrepancySum n f X : ℝ)|) ∧
    63→    -- Upper bound: there exists a ±1 function with all discrepancies bounded
    64→    (∃ f : Fin n → Fin n → ℤ, (∀ i j, f i j = 1 ∨ f i j = -1) ∧
    65→      ∀ X : Finset (Fin n),
    66→        |(discrepancySum n f X : ℝ)| ≤ C₂ * (n : ℝ) ^ ((3 : ℝ) / 2)) := by
    67→  sorry
    68→
    69→end Erdos1028
    70→

    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 954
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/954](https://www.erdosproblems.com/954)
    23→
    24→Let $0 = a_0 < a_1 < a_2 < \cdots$ be the Rosen sequence, where each $a_{k+1}$ is the
    25→smallest integer $n$ such that the number of pairs $(i,j)$ with $0 \le i \le j \le k$,
    26→$j \ge 1$, and $a_i + a_j \le n$ is strictly less than $n$. Is the total pair count
    27→$R(x) = \#\{(i,j) : i \le j,\, j \ge 1,\, a_i + a_j \le x\}$ equal to
    28→$x + O(x^{1/4 + o(1)})$?
    29→
    30→[Er77c] Erdős, P., *Problems and results in combinatorial number theory*, p.71.
    31→-/
    32→
    33→open Finset BigOperators Filter
    34→
    35→namespace Erdos954
    36→
    37→/--
    38→Count of pairs $(i,j)$ with $1 \le j \le k$, $0 \le i \le j$, and $a(i) + a(j) \le n$.
    39→Used in the recursive definition of the Rosen sequence.
    40→-/
    41→def rosenPairCount (a : ℕ → ℕ) (k n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    42→  ∑ j ∈ Finset.range (k + 1),
    43→    if j = 0 then 0
    44→    else ((Finset.range (j + 1)).filter fun i => a i + a j ≤ n).card
    45→
    46→/--
    47→Total count of pairs $(i,j)$ with $i \le j$, $j \ge 1$, from the full infinite
    48→sequence, such that $a(i) + a(j) \le x$. For a strictly increasing sequence
    49→on $\mathbb{N}$, $a(j) \ge j$, so restricting $j \le x$ suffices.
    50→-/
    51→def rosenPairCountTotal (a : ℕ → ℕ) (x : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    52→  ∑ j ∈ Finset.range (x + 1),
    53→    if j = 0 then 0
    54→    else ((Finset.range (j + 1)).filter fun i => a i + a j ≤ x).card
    55→
    56→/--
    57→The Rosen sequence property: $a(0) = 0$, the sequence is strictly increasing,
    58→and each $a(k+1)$ is the smallest integer $n$ such that the pair count with
    59→respect to the first $k+1$ terms is strictly less than $n$.
    60→-/
    61→def IsRosenSequence (a : ℕ → ℕ) : Prop :=
    62→  a 0 = 0 ∧ StrictMono a ∧
    63→  ∀ k, (∀ m, m < a (k + 1) → m ≤ rosenPairCount a k m) ∧
    64→       rosenPairCount a k (a (k + 1)) < a (k + 1)
    65→
    66→/--
    67→Erdős Problem 954 [Er77c, p.71]:
    68→
    69→Let $0 = a_0 < a_1 < a_2 < \cdots$ be the sequence defined by $a_0 = 0$ and $a_{k+1}$ is
    70→the smallest integer $n$ for which the number of pairs $(i,j)$ with
    71→$0 \le i \le j \le k$, $j \ge 1$, and $a_i + a_j \le n$ is strictly less than $n$.
    72→
    73→The sequence begins $0, 1, 3, 5, 9, 13, 17, 24, 31, 38, 45, \ldots$ (OEIS A390642).
    74→
    75→Is the total pair count $R(x) = \#\{(i,j) : i \le j,\, j \ge 1,\, a_i + a_j \le x\}$
    76→equal to $x + O(x^{1/4 + o(1)})$?
    77→
    78→By construction $R(x) \ge x$ always. Erdős and Rosen could not even prove
    79→whether $R(x) \le (1 + o(1))x$.
    80→-/
    81→@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
    82→theorem erdos_954 :
    83→    answer(sorry) ↔
    84→      ∀ a : ℕ → ℕ, IsRosenSequence a →
    85→        ∀ ε : ℝ, 0 < ε →
    86→          ∃ C : ℝ, 0 < C ∧ ∀ᶠ (x : ℕ) in atTop,
    87→            abs ((rosenPairCountTotal a x : ℝ) - (x : ℝ)) ≤
    88→              C * (x : ℝ) ^ ((1 : ℝ) / 4 + ε) := by
    89→  sorry
    90→
    91→end Erdos954
    92→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 983
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/983](https://www.erdosproblems.com/983)
    23→
    24→Let $n \geq 2$ and $\pi(n) < k \leq n$. Let $f(k,n)$ be the smallest integer $r$ such that
    25→in any $A \subseteq \{1,\ldots,n\}$ of size $|A| = k$ there exist primes $p_1,\ldots,p_r$ such
    26→that more than $r$ elements $a \in A$ are only divisible by primes from $\{p_1,\ldots,p_r\}$.
    27→
    28→[Er70b] Erdős, P. and Straus, E.G.
    29→-/
    30→
    31→open Classical
    32→
    33→namespace Erdos983
    34→
    35→/-- The prime counting function $\pi(n)$: the number of primes $\leq n$. -/
    36→def primeCounting (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    37→  ((Finset.range (n + 1)).filter Nat.Prime).card
    38→
    39→/-- A natural number $a$ is smooth with respect to a set of primes $P$ if every
    40→prime factor of $a$ belongs to $P$. -/
    41→def IsSmoothWrt (a : ℕ) (P : Finset ℕ) : Prop :=
    42→  ∀ p : ℕ, Nat.Prime p → p ∣ a → p ∈ P
    43→
    44→/-- $f(k,n)$ is the smallest integer $r$ such that in any $A \subseteq \{1,\ldots,n\}$ of size
    45→$|A| = k$, there exist $r$ primes $p_1,\ldots,p_r$ such that more than $r$ elements of $A$ are
    46→only divisible by primes from $\{p_1,\ldots,p_r\}$. -/
    47→noncomputable def smoothCoveringNumber (k n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    48→  sInf {r : ℕ | ∀ A : Finset ℕ, (∀ a ∈ A, 1 ≤ a ∧ a ≤ n) → A.card = k →
    49→    ∃ P : Finset ℕ, (∀ p ∈ P, Nat.Prime p) ∧ P.card = r ∧
    50→      ∃ B : Finset ℕ, B ⊆ A ∧ B.card > r ∧ ∀ b ∈ B, IsSmoothWrt b P}
    51→
    52→/--
    53→Erdős Problem 983 [Er70b]:
    54→
    55→Is it true that $2\pi(\sqrt{n}) - f(\pi(n)+1, n) \to \infty$ as $n \to \infty$?
    56→
    57→Formulated as: for every $M$, there exists $N_0$ such that for all $n \geq N_0$,
    58→$f(\pi(n)+1, n) + M \leq 2\pi(\lfloor\sqrt{n}\rfloor)$.
    59→-/
    60→@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
    61→theorem erdos_983 :
    62→    answer(sorry) ↔
    63→    ∀ M : ℕ, ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N₀ →
    64→      smoothCoveringNumber (primeCounting n + 1) n + M ≤ 2 * primeCounting (Nat.sqrt n) := by
    65→  sorry
    66→
    67→end Erdos983
    68→

    16→import Mathlib.Data.Finset.Basic

    20→/-!
    21→# Hypergraph Ramsey Numbers
    22→
    23→This file defines the `r`-uniform hypergraph Ramsey number `R_r(n)`.
    24→-/
    25→
    26→namespace Combinatorics
    27→
    28→/--
    29→The `r`-uniform hypergraph Ramsey number `R_r(n)`.
    30→The smallest natural number `m` such that every 2-coloring of the `r`-subsets of a set of size `m`
    31→contains a monochromatic subset of size `n`.
    32→-/
    33→noncomputable def hypergraphRamsey (r n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    34→  sInf { m | ∀ (c : Finset (Fin m) → Bool),
    35→    ∃ (S : Finset (Fin m)), S.card = n ∧
    36→      ∃ (color : Bool), ∀ (e : Finset (Fin m)), e ⊆ S → e.card = r → c e = color }
    37→
    38→/-- `n ≤ hypergraphRamsey r n` when the set is nonempty. -/
    39→theorem le_hypergraphRamsey (r n : ℕ) (hne : { m | ∀ (c : Finset (Fin m) → Bool),
    40→    ∃ (S : Finset (Fin m)), S.card = n ∧
    41→      ∃ (color : Bool), ∀ (e : Finset (Fin m)), e ⊆ S → e.card = r → c e = color }.Nonempty) :
    42→    n ≤ hypergraphRamsey r n := by
    43→  apply le_csInf hne
    44→  intro m hm
    45→  have : ∃ (S : Finset (Fin m)), S.card = n ∧ _ := hm (fun _ ↦ false)
    46→  obtain ⟨S, hS, -⟩ := this
    47→  calc n = S.card := hS.symm
    48→    _ ≤ Fintype.card (Fin m) := Finset.card_le_univ S
    49→    _ = m := Fintype.card_fin m
    50→
    51→/-- `hypergraphRamsey r r = r` for any `r`. -/
    52→theorem hypergraphRamsey_self (r : ℕ) : hypergraphRamsey r r = r := by
    53→  have h_mem : r ∈ { m | ∀ (c : Finset (Fin m) → Bool),
    54→      ∃ (S : Finset (Fin m)), S.card = m ∧
    55→        ∃ (color : Bool), ∀ (e : Finset (Fin m)), e ⊆ S → e.card = r → c e = color } := by
    56→    intro c
    57→    refine ⟨Finset.univ, ?_, c Finset.univ, ?_⟩
    58→    · simp [Fintype.card_fin]
    59→    · intro e _ he_card
    60→      congr 1
    61→      exact Finset.eq_univ_of_card e (he_card.trans (Fintype.card_fin r).symm)
    62→  exact le_antisymm (Nat.sInf_le h_mem) (le_hypergraphRamsey r r ⟨r, h_mem⟩)
    63→
    64→end Combinatorics

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 608
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/608](https://www.erdosproblems.com/608)
    23→
    24→Let $G$ be a graph with $n$ vertices and more than $n^2/4$ edges. Are there at least
    25→$(2/9)n^2$ edges of $G$ which are contained in a $C_5$?
    26→
    27→This was disproved. Füredi and Maleki constructed graphs with more than $n^2/4$ edges
    28→where at most $c \cdot n^2 + O(n)$ edges are in a $C_5$, with
    29→$c = (2 + \sqrt{2})/16 \approx 0.2134$. Grzesik, Hu, and Volec [GHV19] proved this is
    30→optimal: any graph with more than $n^2/4$ edges contains at least $(c - o(1)) \cdot n^2$
    31→edges in a $C_5$.
    32→
    33→[EFR92] Erdős, Faudree, Rousseau (1992)
    34→
    35→[Er97d] Erdős (1997)
    36→
    37→[GHV19] Grzesik, A., Hu, P., and Volec, J.
    38→-/
    39→
    40→open scoped Classical
    41→open SimpleGraph
    42→
    43→namespace Erdos608
    44→
    45→/-- The number of edges of $G$ contained in some $5$-cycle. An edge $\{u, v\}$ is
    46→in a $C_5$ if there exist vertices $w_1, w_2, w_3$ (all five pairwise distinct)
    47→such that $u$-$w_1$-$w_2$-$w_3$-$v$-$u$ is a $5$-cycle in $G$. Edges are counted as
    48→ordered pairs $(u, v)$ with $u < v$ to avoid double-counting. -/
    49→noncomputable def numEdgesInC5 {n : ℕ} (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)) : ℕ :=
    50→  (Finset.univ.filter fun p : Fin n × Fin n =>
    51→    p.1 < p.2 ∧ G.Adj p.1 p.2 ∧
    52→    ∃ w₁ w₂ w₃ : Fin n,
    53→      ({p.1, p.2, w₁, w₂, w₃} : Finset (Fin n)).card = 5 ∧
    54→      G.Adj p.1 w₁ ∧ G.Adj w₁ w₂ ∧ G.Adj w₂ w₃ ∧ G.Adj w₃ p.2).card
    55→
    56→/--
    57→**Erdős Problem 608** (Disproved) [EFR92, Er97d]:
    58→
    59→If $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices with more than $n^2/4$ edges, then at least
    60→$(2/9) \cdot n^2$ edges of $G$ are contained in a $C_5$.
    61→
    62→This is false. The correct threshold is $c = (2+\sqrt{2})/16 \approx 0.2134$, proved
    63→optimal by Grzesik, Hu, and Volec [GHV19].

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 573
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/573](https://www.erdosproblems.com/573)
    23→
    24→Is it true that $\operatorname{ex}(n; \{C_3, C_4\}) \sim (n/2)^{3/2}$?
    25→
    26→[Er71] Erdős, P., *Topics in combinatorial analysis*, 1971, p.103.
    27→[Er75] Erdős, P., *Problems and results on combinatorial number theory*, 1975.
    28→[ErSi82] Erdős, P. and Simonovits, M., 1982.
    29→[Er93] Erdős, P., 1993, p.336.
    30→-/
    31→
    32→open Filter
    33→
    34→open scoped Topology Real
    35→
    36→namespace Erdos573
    37→
    38→/-- A simple graph is triangle-free ($C_3$-free) if no three vertices are mutually adjacent. -/
    39→def TriangleFree {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) : Prop :=
    40→  ∀ u v w : V, G.Adj u v → G.Adj v w → ¬G.Adj u w
    41→
    42→/-- A simple graph is $C_4$-free if it contains no 4-cycle.
    43→The conditions $a \neq c$ and $b \neq d$ ensure the four vertices are distinct
    44→(the remaining distinctness conditions follow from irreflexivity of `Adj`). -/
    45→def C4Free {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) : Prop :=
    46→  ∀ a b c d : V, a ≠ c → b ≠ d →
    47→    G.Adj a b → G.Adj b c → G.Adj c d → ¬G.Adj d a
    48→
    49→/-- The extremal number $\operatorname{ex}(n; \{C_3, C_4\})$: the maximum number of edges in a
    50→simple graph on $n$ vertices containing neither a triangle nor a 4-cycle. -/
    51→noncomputable def exC3C4 (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    52→  sSup {k : ℕ | ∃ G : SimpleGraph (Fin n),
    53→    TriangleFree G ∧ C4Free G ∧ G.edgeSet.ncard = k}
    54→
    55→/--
    56→Is it true that $\operatorname{ex}(n; \{C_3, C_4\}) \sim (n/2)^{3/2}$?
    57→
    58→The extremal number $\operatorname{ex}(n; \{C_3, C_4\})$ is the maximum number of edges in a graph
    59→on $n$ vertices that contains no triangle ($C_3$) and no 4-cycle ($C_4$).
    60→
    61→The conjecture asserts that this quantity is asymptotically $(n/2)^{3/2}$,
    62→i.e. the ratio $\operatorname{ex}(n; \{C_3, C_4\}) / (n/2)^{3/2}$ tends to $1$ as $n \to \infty$.
    63→
    64→Erdős and Simonovits proved that $\operatorname{ex}(n; \{C_4, C_5\}) = (n/2)^{3/2} + O(n)$.
    65→Kővári, Sós, and Turán proved that the extremal number for forbidding $C_4$
    66→together with any odd cycle is $\sim (n/2)^{3/2}$. This problem asks whether
    67→the same holds when only $C_3$ (triangles) are forbidden alongside $C_4$.
    68→
    69→References: [Er71,p.103], [Er75], [ErSi82], [Er93,p.336]
    70→-/
    71→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    72→theorem erdos_573 :
    73→    answer(sorry) ↔
    74→      Tendsto
    75→        (fun n : ℕ => (exC3C4 n : ℝ) / ((n : ℝ) / 2) ^ ((3 : ℝ) / 2))
    76→        atTop (nhds 1) := by
    77→  sorry
    78→
    79→end Erdos573
